title: Gas Station
code: static int CanCompleteCircuit(int[] gas, int[] cost)
tank += diff;                   // surplus since current candidate start
return total >= 0 ? start : -1;     // start valid only if total fuel suffices

cost.label: cost (fuel to reach the next station)

frames.caption: Initialize total = 0, tank = 0, start = 0. The first candidate start is station 0.
frames.note: Two accumulators do all the work. `total` is the GLOBAL surplus across every station — at the end, total >= 0 is the exact feasibility test (enough fuel exists somewhere to make the loop). `tank` is the LOCAL surplus since the current candidate start; it answers "can I get from start to here without running dry?". They diverge only because tank gets reset and total never does.

frames.caption: i = 0: diff = gas[0] − cost[0] = 1 − 3 = -2.
frames.note: diff = gas[i] − cost[i] is the net fuel change at station i: positive means you leave with a surplus, negative means this leg costs more than it gives. The whole problem collapses onto this one number per station — we never simulate driving the full loop from every start (that is the O(n²) trap), we just sweep the diffs once.

frames.caption: total += -2 = -2; tank += -2 = -2. tank is the running surplus since start = 0.
frames.note: Two accumulators do all the work. `total` is the GLOBAL surplus across every station — at the end, total >= 0 is the exact feasibility test (enough fuel exists somewhere to make the loop). `tank` is the LOCAL surplus since the current candidate start; it answers "can I get from start to here without running dry?". They diverge only because tank gets reset and total never does.

frames.caption: tank = -2 < 0: stranded somewhere in [0..0] — abandon every start in that range.
frames.note: When tank < 0 we ran dry somewhere in [start..i], so NO station in that range can be the answer: any intermediate j started with even less accumulated surplus on arrival, so it strands at least as early. The exchange argument lets us discard the entire range in one move — jump start to i+1 and zero the tank. That single safe skip is what turns the O(n²) "try every start" simulation into one O(n) pass.

frames.caption: Reset: start = i + 1 = 1, tank = 0. The next station becomes the fresh candidate start.
frames.note: When tank < 0 we ran dry somewhere in [start..i], so NO station in that range can be the answer: any intermediate j started with even less accumulated surplus on arrival, so it strands at least as early. The exchange argument lets us discard the entire range in one move — jump start to i+1 and zero the tank. That single safe skip is what turns the O(n²) "try every start" simulation into one O(n) pass.

frames.caption: i = 1: diff = gas[1] − cost[1] = 2 − 4 = -2.
frames.note: diff = gas[i] − cost[i] is the net fuel change at station i: positive means you leave with a surplus, negative means this leg costs more than it gives. The whole problem collapses onto this one number per station — we never simulate driving the full loop from every start (that is the O(n²) trap), we just sweep the diffs once.

frames.caption: total += -2 = -4; tank += -2 = -2. tank is the running surplus since start = 1.
frames.note: Two accumulators do all the work. `total` is the GLOBAL surplus across every station — at the end, total >= 0 is the exact feasibility test (enough fuel exists somewhere to make the loop). `tank` is the LOCAL surplus since the current candidate start; it answers "can I get from start to here without running dry?". They diverge only because tank gets reset and total never does.

frames.caption: tank = -2 < 0: stranded somewhere in [1..1] — abandon every start in that range.
frames.note: When tank < 0 we ran dry somewhere in [start..i], so NO station in that range can be the answer: any intermediate j started with even less accumulated surplus on arrival, so it strands at least as early. The exchange argument lets us discard the entire range in one move — jump start to i+1 and zero the tank. That single safe skip is what turns the O(n²) "try every start" simulation into one O(n) pass.

frames.caption: Reset: start = i + 1 = 2, tank = 0. The next station becomes the fresh candidate start.
frames.note: When tank < 0 we ran dry somewhere in [start..i], so NO station in that range can be the answer: any intermediate j started with even less accumulated surplus on arrival, so it strands at least as early. The exchange argument lets us discard the entire range in one move — jump start to i+1 and zero the tank. That single safe skip is what turns the O(n²) "try every start" simulation into one O(n) pass.

frames.caption: i = 2: diff = gas[2] − cost[2] = 3 − 5 = -2.
frames.note: diff = gas[i] − cost[i] is the net fuel change at station i: positive means you leave with a surplus, negative means this leg costs more than it gives. The whole problem collapses onto this one number per station — we never simulate driving the full loop from every start (that is the O(n²) trap), we just sweep the diffs once.

frames.caption: total += -2 = -6; tank += -2 = -2. tank is the running surplus since start = 2.
frames.note: Two accumulators do all the work. `total` is the GLOBAL surplus across every station — at the end, total >= 0 is the exact feasibility test (enough fuel exists somewhere to make the loop). `tank` is the LOCAL surplus since the current candidate start; it answers "can I get from start to here without running dry?". They diverge only because tank gets reset and total never does.

frames.caption: tank = -2 < 0: stranded somewhere in [2..2] — abandon every start in that range.
frames.note: When tank < 0 we ran dry somewhere in [start..i], so NO station in that range can be the answer: any intermediate j started with even less accumulated surplus on arrival, so it strands at least as early. The exchange argument lets us discard the entire range in one move — jump start to i+1 and zero the tank. That single safe skip is what turns the O(n²) "try every start" simulation into one O(n) pass.

frames.caption: Reset: start = i + 1 = 3, tank = 0. The next station becomes the fresh candidate start.
frames.note: When tank < 0 we ran dry somewhere in [start..i], so NO station in that range can be the answer: any intermediate j started with even less accumulated surplus on arrival, so it strands at least as early. The exchange argument lets us discard the entire range in one move — jump start to i+1 and zero the tank. That single safe skip is what turns the O(n²) "try every start" simulation into one O(n) pass.

frames.caption: i = 3: diff = gas[3] − cost[3] = 4 − 1 = 3.
frames.note: diff = gas[i] − cost[i] is the net fuel change at station i: positive means you leave with a surplus, negative means this leg costs more than it gives. The whole problem collapses onto this one number per station — we never simulate driving the full loop from every start (that is the O(n²) trap), we just sweep the diffs once.

frames.caption: total += 3 = -3; tank += 3 = 3. tank is the running surplus since start = 3.
frames.note: Two accumulators do all the work. `total` is the GLOBAL surplus across every station — at the end, total >= 0 is the exact feasibility test (enough fuel exists somewhere to make the loop). `tank` is the LOCAL surplus since the current candidate start; it answers "can I get from start to here without running dry?". They diverge only because tank gets reset and total never does.

frames.caption: tank = 3 >= 0: still afloat — start = 3 survives, keep banking surplus.
frames.note: tank stayed >= 0, so the current candidate start is still alive: we have reached station i without running dry. We keep extending the same candidate, banking the surplus, until either the scan ends (this start wins) or the tank goes negative (abandon and jump ahead).

frames.caption: i = 4: diff = gas[4] − cost[4] = 5 − 2 = 3.
frames.note: diff = gas[i] − cost[i] is the net fuel change at station i: positive means you leave with a surplus, negative means this leg costs more than it gives. The whole problem collapses onto this one number per station — we never simulate driving the full loop from every start (that is the O(n²) trap), we just sweep the diffs once.

frames.caption: total += 3 = 0; tank += 3 = 6. tank is the running surplus since start = 3.
frames.note: Two accumulators do all the work. `total` is the GLOBAL surplus across every station — at the end, total >= 0 is the exact feasibility test (enough fuel exists somewhere to make the loop). `tank` is the LOCAL surplus since the current candidate start; it answers "can I get from start to here without running dry?". They diverge only because tank gets reset and total never does.

frames.caption: tank = 6 >= 0: still afloat — start = 3 survives, keep banking surplus.
frames.note: tank stayed >= 0, so the current candidate start is still alive: we have reached station i without running dry. We keep extending the same candidate, banking the surplus, until either the scan ends (this start wins) or the tank goes negative (abandon and jump ahead).

frames.caption: Loop done: total = 0 >= 0, so a solution exists — return start = 3.
frames.note: The final answer hinges only on `total`. If total >= 0 the leftover surplus guarantees the last surviving `start` completes the full circle — the resets proved every earlier start fails, and feasibility proves this one succeeds, so the solution is unique. If total < 0 there is simply not enough fuel anywhere and we return −1.
